rulings.Case Title: PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA VS. PROPERTY SUBJECT TO DISPOSITION: $2,235.87 U.S. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Final Judgment As To Interested Party Steven Terrell Only
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, July 26, 2018, Line 1, PETITIONER PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA's Motion For Final Judgment As To Interested Party Steven Terrell Only. Petitioner People of the State of California's motion for final judgment as to interested party Steven Terrell only is granted. No opposition filed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the court will sign the proposed order and proposed judgment submitted with the moving papers. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA VS. PROPERTY SUBJECT TO DISPOSITION: $860.OO U.S. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order For Terminating Sanctions And Striking Claim
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday July 26, 2018, Line 1, PETITIONER PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA'S Motion For Order For Terminating Sanctions And Striking Claim. Pro Tem Judge Roger Mead, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Petitioner's unopposed motion for terminating sanctions striking the claim of Matthew Davis is granted. If the tentative ruling is not contested, no appearance is required and the Court will sign the proposed order submitted with the motion, but striking the final paragraph (which provides that Petitioner "be allowed" to withdraw the Petition for Forfeiture in Rem and proceed with non-judicial forfeiture proceedings), because the motion papers do not explain the grounds for seeking such relief (or why it is necessary). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rmead@folgerlevin.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: BRENDA GISI VS. HUDDLE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, July 26, 2018, Line 11, DEFENDANT HUDDLE, INC.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication. Defendant Huddle, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is denied and its alternative motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the third, fifth, sixth, and eighth causes of action for age discrimination, failure to prevent discrimination, and violation of the UCL and granted as to the fourth and seventh causes of action for retaliation. Ms. Gisi satisfied her light burden of stating a prima facie case of age discrimination by showing that there are circumstances suggesting a discriminatory motive such as Mr. Roberts' remarks about what kind of people he needed and Ms. Gisi's accounts were distributed to team members significantly younger than her. Ms. Gisi also satisfied her burden of showing that Huddle's proffered legitimate reason for terminating Ms. Gisi (the need to lay off employees with poor performance) was pretextual based on the following evidence liberally construed in her favor: (1) the remarks of Mr. Brogger to Ms. Gisi's former supervisor that, while he wanted to retain Ms. Herrington who was "young and pretty and energetic" he wondered whether she had gone "to the dinosaur graveyard to find [her] team" of which Ms. Gisi was a member; (2) Mr. Roberts' remarks to Ms. Herrington that he needed "'fast ¿sharp people . . . with a clean slate'. . .and not burdened with years of experience elsewhere" (3) Mr. Roberts' differential treatment of the older sales representatives compared to the younger ones; (4) inconsistencies in Huddle's explanations as to how and when the decision to terminate plaintiff was made; and (5) there was a significant change of focus of Ms. Gisi's work which negatively affected her ability to achieve her sales quota. This evidence and Huddle's failure to take action in response to Ms. Gisi's complaint to Ms. Murray precludes granting summary adjudication on the third, fifth, sixth and eighth causes of action. (Reid v. Google, Inc. (2010) 50 Cal.4th 512, 541 (rejecting the "stray remarks" doctrine because "[d]etermining the weight of discriminatory or ambiguous remarks is a role reserved for the jury.")). Huddle is entitled to summary adjudication of the retaliation claims because the undisputed evidence shows there is no causal connection between Ms. Gisi's protected activity and her termination: Mr. Roberts was not aware that Ms. Gisi had complained about his discriminatory treatment of Ms. Gisi and the older employees and Mr. Roberts made the decision to terminate Ms. Gisi's employment before Huddle received Ms. Gisi's email complaining of age discrimination. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Gisi is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: SAN SAN AUNG VS. REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA AT SAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Mental Examination Of Plaintiff
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday July 26, 2018, Line 7, DEFENDANTS JESSEE HERMOGENO, THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA'S Motion To Compel Mental Examination Of Plaintiff. Pro Tem Judge Roger Mead, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant Regents' unopposed motion to compel the mental examination of plaintiff San San Aung pursuant to CCP § 2032.310-2032.340 is granted as follows: Plaintiff is to appear for examination and testing (as described in paragraphs 5 and 6 of the supporting Declaration of Jeffrey Gould, M.D. dated July 2, 2018) on August 3, 2018, beginning at 10:00 a.m., or at such other date and time as the parties and Dr. Gould may agree. The examination is limited to 8 hours, exclusive of breaks. The examination is to be continuously audiotaped by Dr. Gould, including break times, and Dr. Gould is to provide a copy of the recording to plaintiff's counsel. rulings.Case Title: ANNA GATTI VS. LOOP AI LAB INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, July 26, 2018, Line 13, CROSS DEFENDANT ANNA GATTI's MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT. Cross-defendant Anna Gatti's anti-SLAPP motion to strike Loop AI Labs, Inc.'s first cause of action for malicious prosecution in the first amended cross-complaint is granted. The court exercises its discretion per CCP 425.16(f) to hear this motion even though Ms. Gatti filed the motion more than 60 days after service of the original cross-complaint, which alleged a malicious prosecution claim. Ms. Gatti acted diligently in light of the parties' meet and confer discussions. The facts of this case bear no resemblance to Newport Harbor Ventures, LLC v. Morris Cerullo World Evangelism (2018) 4 Cal.5th 637. Addressing the motion will further the purpose of the anti-SLAPP statute to quickly dispose of SLAPP suits. Ms. Gatti demonstrates that the malicious prosecution claim is based on petitioning activity and thus she has satisfied her first prong burden. Loop has not established its second prong burden of showing that its malicious prosecution has minimal merit. "[I]n order to establish a cause of action for malicious prosecution of either a criminal or civil proceeding, a plaintiff must demonstrate 'that the prior action (1) was commenced by or at the direction of the defendant and was pursued to a legal termination in his, plaintiff's, favor; (2) was brought without probable cause; and (3) was initiated with malice.'" (Sheldon Appel Co. v. Albert & Oliker (1989) 47 Cal.3d 863, 871(citations omitted)). Loop cannot satisfy the element of favorable termination. "[T]hat element requires that the termination of the prior action reflect on the merits and tend[ ] to indicate [the former defendant's] innocence of or lack of responsibility for the alleged misconduct. Thus, [a] voluntary dismissal on technical grounds, such as lack of jurisdiction, laches, the statute of limitations or prematurity, does not constitute a favorable termination because it does not reflect on the substantive merits of the underlying claim." (Drummond v. Desmarais (2009) 176 Cal.App.4th 439, 455-456 (internal quotations and citations omitted).) The federal judgment shows that Ms. Gatti's federal counterclaim was dismissed because the federal court declined to exercise supplemental jurisdiction over it. That judgment does not reflect on the merits of the counterclaim. Loop's argument that the counterclaim was abandoned when Ms. Gatti filed an amended answer or answer to the second amended complaint that did not re-plead the counterclaim lacks merit. The abandonment theory is inconsistent with the express language of the federal judgment. Moreover, Ms. Gatti understandably did not re-plead the counterclaim. On October 3, 2015 Loop answered the counterclaim. Ms. Gatti filed the amended answer or answer to second amended complaint on October 7, 2015. Since she was not amending her counterclaim and because Loop already answered it, there was nothing to re-plead. The authorities that characterize a counterclaim as independent from an answer and not waived when not re-pled in a subsequent answer are better reasoned than the authorities relied on by Loop. (Ground Zero Museum Workshop v. Wilson (D. Md. 2011) 813 F.Supp.2d 678, 706.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Gatti is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: ANNA GATTI VS. LOOP AI LAB INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, July 26, 2018, Line 13, CROSS DEFENDANT ANNA GATTI's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT. Cross-defendant Anna Gatti's demurrer to both causes of action in Loop AI Labs, Inc.'s first amended cross-complaint is moot as to the first cause of action for malicious prosecution in light of the ruling on the Ms. Gatti's anti-SLAPP motion and overruled as to the second cause of action for enforcement of judgment. An order for the payment of money can be considered a judgment for collection and enforcement purposes. (CCP 680.230 and 680.270). Loop properly filed this action to add Ms. Gatti as an alter ego on the "judgment." (Highland Springs Conference & Training Center v. City of Banning (2016) 244 Cal.App.4th 267, 288.) Judge Gilliam's termination order did not clearly extinguish the prior fee orders. There is no point in staying this case pending resolution of the appeal before the Ninth Circuit because the propriety of the fee orders is not part of the appeal. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Loop is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A. VS. BURRILL M GRAY
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Deem Admissions And Of Nonappearance
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday July 26, 2018, Line 10, PLAINTIFF WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A.'S Motion To Deem Admissions And Of Nonappearance. Pro Tem Judge Roger Mead, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff Wells Fargo Bank's unopposed motion is granted. Plaintiff's Requests for Admission, Set One, Nos. 1 - 10, date April 23, 2018, ([to be] attached [to the order] as Exhibit A) are each deemed admitted by Defendant Burrill M. Gray pursuant to CCP § 2033.280. [If the tentative is unopposed, Plaintiff's counsel need not appear and may submit the proposed order (omitting bracketed portions of the tentative, and attaching a copy of the Requests for Admissions) by e-mail to the Judge Pro Tem. Plaintiff's counsel is reminded of the requirements of Local Rule 2.7B regarding the lodging of courtesy copies and the potential consequences of failing to do so.] Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rmead@folgerlevin.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
